speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you about where you grew up in Greece, what it was like

speaker: SS
words: where you grew up.

speaker: GD
words: Well, if I tell you the truth, it was conditions fifty years ago and

speaker: GD
words: now, it's better, a little bit better. Where I grew up, it was a village

speaker: GD
words: oh, three hundred people, maybe more, I don't know. We didn't have nothing

speaker: GD
words: you know, convenience of any kind. There were no water in houses, you

speaker: GD
words: have to pull the water from the well, you know, to use it in houses.

speaker: GD
words: And I don't know, conditions were pretty bad then. But they lived

speaker: GD
words: through and they stayed bad. I was over there about ten, twelve years

speaker: GD
words: ago and they haven't changed much. The only thing they have now is trans

speaker: GD
words: portation. You see, the United States went overe there and built a lot

speaker: GD
words: of roads, and they have the transportation now. But they didn't have

speaker: GD
words: very much convenience now, electricity, but, as I say, there's no water

speaker: GD
words: in house, no electricity. They have to light themselves with the lamps.

speaker: GD
words: They have lamps now, at the time when I grew up they had oil, olive

speaker: GD
words: oil and they have pot in there where they put the oil and then they

speaker: GD
words: light the wick. They light the wick you see, and that's the way they,

speaker: GD
words: but now they have what they call the gas, you see, they pump it. They

speaker: GD
words: have them, gas lamps. Not everybody, but those that are in stores and

speaker: GD
words: things like that, but not in private houses.

speaker: SS
words: Was it farming there?

speaker: GD
words: Mcstly farming, yes. The terrain is bad, you know, they have a little

speaker: GD
words: bit of a land in the flat, there is a flat, I don't know how many miles

speaker: GD
words: and that's where they raise their wheat and everything. They used to

speaker: GD
words: raise potatoes and they were the best because I guess they got the seed

speaker: GD
words: from Germany. They raise good potatoes, that's one good thing. But all

speaker: GD
words: the other things, they have probably some of those, but they didn't have much land,

speaker: GD
words: they had

speaker: GD
words: to dig in the sides of the mountain, you see, you know what I mean?

speaker: GD
words: The mountainside, we had to dig and find water, then kind of dam it

speaker: GD
words: there with the rocks. And they use the well for garden or any

speaker: GD
words: thing, but they didn't have no water. There wasn't much rain. But now

speaker: GD
words: they have different system, because they are well enough to buy gas to

speaker: GD
words: pump the water to irrigate the land.

speaker: SS
words: When you grew up there, was there much future for you?

speaker: GD
words: There wasn't any future of any kind. When I grew up— when I left the

speaker: GD
words: school— oh, about a year after I left the school- I was getting differ

speaker: GD
words: ent jobs and my father sent me to an island, where he had a cousin, you

speaker: GD
words: know, and he had a bakery. He was baking bread and I worked

speaker: GD
words: for three years before I came to this country.

speaker: SS
words: How far was that from home?

speaker: GD
words: That was about Sixteen miles. You couldn't leave there, you had to have

speaker: GD
words: a boat or something to cross the water. And, of course, in other places

speaker: GD
words: where they sent me, I get homesick and I go home.

speaker: SS
words: What did you work for in the bakery? Did you make money; did they give

speaker: SS
words: you money?

speaker: GD
words: Well, the first few years, the first two years, I didn't get any money.

speaker: GD
words: Then he used to give flour But after that, I drew salary. He give me so much a month

speaker: GD
words: and I had

speaker: GD
words: to eat from that and do everything else, although I had free laundry

speaker: GD
words: and free clothing and shoes. But he did give me so much money.

speaker: SS
words: Did the flour go to the family?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. yeah.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you when you started working for him?

speaker: GD
words: How old?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: GD
words: Let's see, I was about thirteen, fourteen years old. Because I worked

speaker: GD
words: in different places in town there and I get homesick and go home.

speaker: SS
words: When did you leave school?

speaker: GD
words: School, four years. I had four years of school. Six and four is ten

speaker: GD
words: years. Ten years old when I left the school. And then I worked threefour

speaker: GD
words: years in different places, sometimes I didn t work, id stay

speaker: GD
words: home. And finally they sent me over to this island and I couldn't get

speaker: GD
words: away from there, see?

speaker: SS
words: You didn't like it too much?

speaker: GD
words: I didn't like it very well, but I got used— it wasn't very pleasant at

speaker: GD
words: first, but then I got used—I think I liked it, it was better. Then I

speaker: GD
words: was gone, you know, it was better than at home I had nothing to look

speaker: GD
words: tor, you see; nothing to eat,

speaker: SS
words: How many kids in the family with you?

speaker: GD
words: Seven. Five boys and two girls.

speaker: GD
words: What is it that was bringing people to America? Did they hear much

speaker: GD
words: about America over there?

speaker: GD
words: Yes, sure. When I left there they had been hearing America, six,

speaker: GD
words: seven, years, eight. They all heard about America, but they couldn't

speaker: GD
words: get enough money to buy a ticket to leave. But they heard about America,

speaker: GD
words: yes. Sure they heard. And they thought that America was Well, Am

speaker: GD
words: erica was a good country, and it is still good country, But they come

speaker: GD
words: here with the intention to make money and go back and live there, and

speaker: GD
words: a lot of 'em did. But, I, myself, I never had the—After I lived out

speaker: GD
words: to here, I didn't want to go back. From here, my cousins, some of my

speaker: GD
words: cousins went back, got married. They raised a family- they had

speaker: GD
words: a few dollars from here, see. But that way some succeeded and some

speaker: GD
words: failed. Some bought land, you see, with the money they brought from

speaker: GD
words: here, and they were living pretty good. But others, others they didn't.

speaker: GD
words: It's the same as in America. Some succeed and others fail. Not every

speaker: GD
words: body get rich, you see.

speaker: SS
words: Was most of what people heard in Greece, that Anerica was a land of

speaker: SS
words: opportunity?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. And they came with the purpose to get the money, but, of course,

speaker: GD
words: a lot of 'em didn't. A lot of 'em stayed here, see? There was many,

speaker: GD
words: many Greeks here, havere, they're in position to you know Pantages,

speaker: GD
words: who had the theaters? He was a Greek, Another feller here in business

speaker: GD
words: they made big money, they got rich. But Pantages, of course, he, I don't

speaker: GD
words: know what happened to him at the last. He lost everything.

speaker: SS
words: Did people know that there was going to be a lot of work available when

speaker: SS
words: they got here?

speaker: GD
words: Oh yes. Yes, a lot of 'em, a lot of fellows, the Greeks that were here

speaker: GD
words: before and they knew the language, they could speak the language, they

speaker: GD
words: took advantage of them , you see. They make 'em pay money for the jobs.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, you mean like the contractors would hire...?

speaker: GD
words: Yes, but they are not contractors. They were working for the railroad.

speaker: GD
words: There was one fella, he was a head there in the railroad and he had

speaker: GD
words: a contract with the company to supply him with so many men.

speaker: SS
words: He would get people to pay him money for the job, and he cheated 'em?

speaker: GD
words: What do you call that? Not cheat, that's not a very good word.

speaker: SS
words: He was the middleman, that's for sure,

speaker: GD
words: Yeah, he was a ...

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like he conned 'em, a little bit.

speaker: GD
words: Yeah. Anyway, that's what they was doing. Them fellas that were here

speaker: GD
words: before, you know and they understood some of the language, and they

speaker: GD
words: contracted. Of course, in the rest of the gangs where I worked here,

speaker: GD
words: they had a

speaker: GD
words: interpreter. They had a man next to the boss, he wasn't supposed to

speaker: GD
words: do common labor, he was supposed to explain to the men what the boss

speaker: GD
words: had told him, and so he'd have to make them understand themself what

speaker: GD
words: they did— he would tell them what to do. Because the fellas that

speaker: GD
words: work there, they just came from the Old Country, they couldn't speak

speaker: GD
words: the language.

speaker: SS
words: When you came over— Your father came over first?

speaker: SS
words: in the bakery and he finally sent me a ticket, you know, send me a tick-

speaker: GD
words: My father was here a couple of years before I was. I was over forking

speaker: GD
words: et for me and my brother and we came here. We came here to Spokane in

speaker: SS
words: What did you think when you saw this country? What did you think of it?

speaker: GD
words: What I thought of it?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: GD
words: I thought it was alright. The country itself was alright. But the work

speaker: GD
words: on the railroad was terrible, because we were working hard them days.

speaker: GD
words: here, you see my hands were all blistered. I thought then, I thought

speaker: GD
words: if I had the money, I would go right back. I didn't like the job, but

speaker: GD
words: then after that, after a while, I got used to that, and did pretty good.

speaker: SS
words: How did you get on— first you came over to Spokane— Did you get off in

speaker: SS
words: New York?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. We had to get off in New York. I still, today, I don't remember

speaker: GD
words: what deal it was. But they put usNfrom one boat to the other. We were

speaker: GD
words: run through the they called it. That used to be island

speaker: GD
words: in New York, where all the foreigners is coming— I think they sold that

speaker: GD
words: island. Well, anyway, we came through here in New York and they put

speaker: GD
words: us— not in boat for twenty-four hours we traveled by boat. And then

speaker: GD
words: when we got out there, there was a lot of niggers, lots of niggers.

speaker: GD
words: It must have been in the South, because there was a lot of niggers.

speaker: GD
words: And they had to switch the cars on the sidetrack and they had to wait

speaker: GD
words: there for quite a while before they took us— we went up to town and

speaker: GD
words: we found some of the Greek grocery stores, they had grocery stores there.

speaker: GD
words: And we bought some cheese and things from there to eat, because we were

speaker: GD
words: hungry. When we left New York, they give us, oh, bologna and bread and

speaker: GD
words: so on. So many things, you know over there. Some didn't eat that, but

speaker: GD
words: I did, I liked it, too. Pie, one pie you see, not piece, but whole pie.

speaker: GD
words: It was a box and we paid dollar for that box. It was lunch.A it was

speaker: GD
words: plenty of lunch for two, three days. And we got along pretty good.

speaker: SS
words: Then you came all the way— did you come by train?

speaker: GD
words: Yes, we came by trainthet. From there we came on the Northern Pacific

speaker: GD
words: Station in Spokane Burlington. I don't know— Burlington but

speaker: GD
words: it wasn't Burlington ' from the East, and brought us over here to West,

speaker: GD
words: Montana and so on. And once in a while you see Indians, you know. They

speaker: GD
words: used to come on the train, we didn't know what they was, they look funny

speaker: GD
words: dressed in the Indian costumes. And we asked somebody and they say,

speaker: GD
words: "Indians." Erom St. Louis, Missouri, we met some other fellas

speaker: GD
words: were here before, two, three years, you know, and they could speak few

speaker: GD
words: words English, some like Greeks, and we got along fine. They came

speaker: GD
words: to Spokane, they were shaping from there to Spokane through the railroad

speaker: GD
words: they give 'em pass, you see, they didn't have to pay. They get pass

speaker: GD
words: and they were shipped from there to Spokane, and we got into Spokane

speaker: GD
words: just fine and dandy with them, you see, because if we wanted to eat

speaker: GD
words: they knew a few words and we didn't know any. So that was pretty nice.

speaker: SS
words: They got to ship free because they worked on the railroad?

speaker: GD
words: Because they come here to work. They wanted to send 'em to West, you

speaker: GD
words: see, what they called the "Wild West" them days. (Chuckles) They didn't

speaker: GD
words: want to come to West, pu know. The people didn't want to come to West.

speaker: GD
words: They thought that the Eastern towns were better. It wasn't bad when

speaker: GD
words: we came here.

speaker: GD
words: Was this where most of the work was? Out West at this time?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. The Milwaukee was building a new roadto the West. It was hand

speaker: GD
words: work, there was no machinery, like there is today, it was all hand work

speaker: GD
words: and it required a lot of men, to work. Now it is different. We had

speaker: GD
words: to work with pick and shovel and whatever they had. And if they wantedagainst

speaker: GD
words: the mountain or something,they had to blastthe dynamite and

speaker: GD
words: then level it off with the pick and shovel.

speaker: GD
words: Well, how did you get on to work? Did you go to the employment agency?

speaker: GD
words: No. No. we had interpreter and he went and got the job from the employ

speaker: GD
words: ment agency, of course. It was — in Spokane, you know there was a lot—

speaker: GD
words: of employment agencies. And there was a lot of people working there, I

speaker: GD
words: don't know how many. Mostly foreigners. Greeks, Italians, Austrians,

speaker: GD
words: all them countries, you know from over the south of Europe. It wasn't

speaker: GD
words: only Greeks, more Italians than anything else. And Austrians. The Aust

speaker: GD
words: rians, they work mostly in the mines, but they did work on the railroads

speaker: GD
words: too.

speaker: GD
words: Where did you pick up this interpreter? Was he with you— was he some

speaker: GD
words: body you met in America?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah, someone from the same country, you see, but he was here before us

speaker: GD
words: and he could speakEnglish.

speaker: GD
words: Was he one of the guys you met in St. Louis?

speaker: GD
words: No, no, he was West. When we came here, there was two, three of those

speaker: GD
words: fellows, you know, that acting as interpreters. And a lot of

speaker: GD
words: 'em would take a bunch and go out the same, you see. They

speaker: GD
words: were looking for a better job, you see. If we went some place and

speaker: GD
words: didn't like it, why, we quit and go someplace else. But we very seldom

speaker: GD
words: did that, because My first job was in Walla Walla. We were build

speaker: GD
words: ing a spur track to go into a cannery, where they were canning fruit.

speaker: GD
words: To go into the cannery and build a track to go into the cannery. Can

speaker: GD
words: nery was new, you know. And, of course, they were building tracks

speaker: GD
words: not canneries, but—

words: How was the work organized? Did you have a little stretch that you did

speaker: SS
words: all by yourself, or how did they do it?

speaker: GD
words: No, no, they all together. The railroad you neverAby yourself.

speaker: GD
words: You— leveling the ground, and of course, we had to shovel the dirtthe

speaker: GD
words: surplus dirt, you know that was there and haul it away on push cars.

speaker: GD
words: Had a car run on the railroad which they hauled the push car and they

speaker: GD
words: loading,—-No, you never worked along with the railroad. When you lay

speaker: GD
words: the ties down, then you lay the steel on the top of them, then, of course

speaker: GD
words: you got to learn to spike them and everything, so the boxcars and so on

speaker: GD
words: they could go on, you see. It was altogether work, that is, they didn't

speaker: GD
words: do it alone, by themselves.

speaker: SS
words: What did you make? What was the wages?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, the wages— the wages was a dollar and thirty cents a day.

speaker: SS
words: How many hours was that for?

speaker: GD
words: Ten hours. Some were a dollar and a half; some a dollar thirty-five.

speaker: GD
words: And, of course, there was jobs in different places where they was getting

speaker: GD
words: more, probably two dollars a day. But they were contracts, you see, and

speaker: GD
words: over there you have to— they already had taken the job.

speaker: SS
words: Taken? The other fellows. There was other people.The le-rprie and

speaker: GD
words: so on and so forth.

speaker: SS
words: Was it hard work?

speaker: GD
words: Hard work, sure, I'll say it was hard work. It was labor by hand—

speaker: GD
words: ways is hard work. Then the train would come 'round, and work there

speaker: GD
words: and hook around and fill all the gravel', fill the inside of the—be-

speaker: GD
words: cause there were dump earss you see. Some of the cars they were dumping

speaker: GD
words: right in the middle of the track. Well, you had to shovel by hand off

speaker: GD
words: the track, where it was not needed, you see. It was alright if you have

speaker: GD
words: to raise the tracks high enough to put it under there, but where it was

speaker: GD
words: not needed, why, snovel it off—.'cause it wasn't level, it just was up

speaker: GD
words: and down.

words: Oh, yeah, we had a— boxcars. Well, not exactly boxcars, they were box

words: cars, but they were made for people to live in. And they had windows

words: and so on. And that's where we slept. We had to build our own beds and

words: everything. In some they were already built. Let s see, two, tour,

speaker: GD
words: four is eight— sixteen people in one boxcar. See they had bunks,

speaker: GD
words: of four-

speaker: GD
words: One up and one down, you see. They had two layers. That way while we

speaker: GD
words: worked. If we had to move four, five miles,ten miles or a hundred miles

speaker: GD
words: the box cars with us. One time, I was the baker, and I had

speaker: GD
words: fcwad 4WWtnd we du8 the coals in the ground for oven. Burn it with

speaker: GD
words: the ties, old ties, you know, and I had just finished the bread and put

speaker: GD
words: it in the oven. It was about half done, and then they come around -

speaker: GD
words: the train come around and he says you move to a certain place. We had

speaker: GD
words: to move to another place, you see, from there, So we had

speaker: GD
words: to diet the bread half done. 'Cause sometimes, you know, we get our order

speaker: GD
words: to move. Sometimes even at nights, they come round and move you away

speaker: GD
words: from— 'course they don't move you away, they move the cars. You are

speaker: GD
words: in the sleeping cars.

speaker: SS
words: I'm interested in that. And because I know a lot of men worked on that

speaker: SS
words: and then worked—

speaker: GD
words: The chief industry then was the railroad, see. 'Cause they're all new

speaker: GD
words: the railroads, see. They're not all the Chicago-Milwaukee, they had a

speaker: GD
words: new— they  West at that time, 1907, it's about a couple of

speaker: GD
words: years before I come, but they was still working up there in Montana.

speaker: GD
words: That pretty rough country over there, see. They had to go through rough

speaker: GD
words: country— pretty expensive business. But they had the money and they

speaker: GD
words: built 'em. Then, of course, they wasn't paying very much, but dollar

words: and a half and two dollars a day. Two dollars a day when I came in the

speaker: SS
words: ing on the railroad?

speaker: GD
words: Well, yes. There were bunches, see. Now one there would have probably

words: thirty men, or forty. One interpreter would have thirty men or forty.

speaker: GD
words: Another interpreter, he probably would have forty Italians or forty

speaker: GD
words: different nationalities. But they were separate. Sometimes they were

speaker: GD
words: together; worked together. But most of the times, they separate.

speaker: SS
words: Were most of the men that you worked with, men that you'd come over with

speaker: SS
words: from Greece?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. Yes, men that I knew. I either come over with them or they were

speaker: GD
words: here before me. Most of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Were many from your own hometown?

speaker: GD
words: Yes, from my hometown.

speaker: SS
words: You started out together then. You came at the same time?

speaker: GD
words: Well, not the same time. Some were here. Some fellas were here, you

speaker: GD
words: know before. They were here maybe two, three years. Two years anyway.

speaker: GD
words: But when we went out to work, they were already working sometime And

speaker: GD
words: we didn't have buy a ticket because you get a pass, you see and they send

speaker: GD
words: you in to the job.

speaker: SS
words: So you met up with some of these guys when you were working?

speaker: GD
words: Yeah. And I knew some of 'em, I know most all of 'em, because they were

speaker: GD
words: from the same town.

words: Did the crew work as one group? You said that if you didn't like it,

speaker: SS
words: you could quit and go someplace else. Would that be the whole crew that

words: would decide that they didn't want it?

speaker: GD
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: GD
words: Yes, once or twice. Ue went to a job out of Coeur d'Alene, I think it

words: was, and they didn't have nothing there, you see. No facilities of any

words: kind, and they had tents to live in. So the whole crew went back and

speaker: GD
words: we found different jobs. There were.inan employment office, you see,

speaker: GD
words: job sometime, you don't get another job right away, you probably have

speaker: GD
words: to wait two, three days or a week, and then something will turn up.

speaker: SS
words: But you never walked off the job when you were working?

speaker: GD
words: No. Now, we didn't do that. We just went to one place that I remember

speaker: GD
words: and they didn't have nothing there, you see, so we had to quit. It was

speaker: GD
words: a railroad, it was a contract, you see. And they were making the grade

speaker: GD
words: for railroad, but it was away from the railroad and we didn't have no,

speaker: GD
words: and they drop us over there and then they left. So we had to hire some

speaker: GD
words: farmers and their wagons to haul our belongings and we walked. I think

speaker: GD
words: it was about ten miles, ua walked that time from the work, I know the

speaker: GD
words: name of the now, I can't think of it.

speaker: SS
words: It was a town, into the town.

speaker: GD
words: It was close in Spokane. Not Mica, it's another little town there. It

speaker: GD
words: was the electric car that took us to Spokane.

speaker: SS
words: Someone that I talked to who also worked on the section told me that, it

speaker: SS
words: was slavery. That's what he called it. He said it was really tough and

speaker: SS
words: really bad.

speaker: GD
words: Well, it was hard work. You have to work, ten hours, you know. And it

speaker: GD
words: was hard work, but you get used to it. You couldn't call it slavery

speaker: GD
words: because you could quit qnytime you wanted to. But slavery is something

speaker: GD
words: that you can't avoid.

speaker: SS
words: Could you save money from that?

speaker: GD
words: Not very much. Things were awful cheap in those days, living, it was

speaker: GD
words: cheap, we had some older fellas you know, that they knew how to save

speaker: GD
words: money and we save that way, a few dollars, but not very much money.

speaker: GD
words: We lived on ten or fifteen dollars a month, and we were getting about

speaker: GD
words: forty or thirty five. Thirty five, dollar and a half, dollar thirty five

speaker: GD
words: cents at one time. And that wasn't very much, they raise that to a dollar

speaker: GD
words: and a half. And we were making about thirty dollars, thirty five dollars

speaker: GD
words: a month or forty, and we saved a bit, but not very much. So the old

speaker: GD
words: fellows that were here before, why, they knew how to manage. Section

speaker: GD
words: is different now. In section you, I worked for section, too, but in

speaker: GD
words: the section you don't have too much to do in wintertime, because the

speaker: GD
words: track is already serviced during the summer and in wintertime, we didn't

speaker: GD
words: have too much to do. But in the summer, yoou worked, they have to erase

speaker: GD
words: raw spots and so on.

speaker: SS
words: That's more maintenance thai, construction.

speaker: GD
words: Well yes, maintenance. It was maintenance crew. That's what the section

speaker: GD
words: is maintenance crew. But they have to work, they're always finding work

words: to do.

words: two hundred miles, or fifty miles. And you don't haue the slides. Sometimes

words: going and what it was going to be. Tell you,"Come on". And we went over

words: That was waist deep mud you were working in")

words: and Italians and they kept

words: and got a job. was living down below. See, I told you about it.

words: you see.

words: And the toilets, were the same way, thoy were separate.

speaker: GD
words: house, the place was long and then they had separate buildings for each

words: place for a toilet. You see, they had small toilet for each one of us,

words: those place. And of course, here, here in town, up in Nob Hill, they

words: there and you do your stuff and then once seek, or twice week, no, onc3

words: and houl 'em away. They were hauling them to the Japanese farmer up

words: the building was pretty long, you know. It wasn 't one, it was two

words: fire, you know, I told you how it start fire-and burned down.

words: stove and he

words: they're not like this here, they were temporary buildings, you know, not

speaker: GD
words: durable buildings.

words: why. That's where they started. And one by one, you see. All the common

words: And it was a lath mill there too, you know. There was a lot of 'em worked

words: had they?

words: up there and do that work. And my mob was semiskilled. You couldn't

words: it to know what to trim, what to cut out and what to let go. You see,

words: tell you.

words: same as I and left here in 1945, I think— 1948, I think he left here.

words: And I was with him because we always spoke in our own language, unless

words: someone would come here other than Greek,; speak English. And it's a

words: wonder sometime how I learned to speak ..English language as well, because

speaker: GD
words: down in sawmill, you can't talk you have to use signs. You talk the

words: sign language in sawmill, because too noisy there, you can't talk. And

words: over here, when I came home I spoke Greek. And sometimes I am wondering

words: how did I learn to speak English so well. I don't know whether I do

words: speak well now, I don't,because too old, and my mind is not steady.

speaker: GD
words: Wanting to Something that you really want to do, because

words: in order to stay in America you had to be able to speak English.

words: after that. And that induced me to learn to speak because I had to.

words: I took interest in work, in Masonic work, I mean. And I liked it. So

speaker: GD
words: I studied.

words: No.

words: you see.(Chuckles) The ' you see, then. I wasn t used

words: 4§aJ

words: but the store, different. Didn't pay the rent at all, they didn't have no money, during

speaker: GD
words: the Depression. Of course,

speaker: GD
words: they let us off, there was only one Greek that was working down here

speaker: GD
words: at the planer, and because he was an oldtimer, they didn't lay him off,

speaker: GD
words: but they cut him down. They let him work one day week, sometime. Some

speaker: GD
words: time, less. One day month. And he was kicking, but others didn't have

speaker: GD
words: any job at all.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you had worked for twenty years by then.

speaker: GD
words: Yes, yes, I had worked for twenty years and I went down and saw Mr.

speaker: GD
words: O'Connor, and O'Connor was the general manager here at that time. You

speaker: GD
words: didn't know him, I don't think. And he told me, "Yes, we want you," he

speaker: GD
words: says,"we want you to go to work, because we know that some of the fellows

speaker: GD
words: that have families are no good, and they can't do the job that you do.

speaker: GD
words: But still, we have— we are looking at the human side." You see it was

speaker: GD
words: humanity that they hired them because of their children. If it wasn't

speaker: GD
words: for their children, they would be out. And, of course, that happened,

speaker: GD
words: as I told you, that fella there that was working in the planer. He

speaker: GD
words: went to work on the section and let me have the job here in the sawmill.

speaker: GD
words: And that's how I got on. And one fella from Hc- UT Two single men

speaker: GD
words: were working and all the others were married men with families.

speaker: SS
words: So you— was this when the Depression was ending or still going?

speaker: GD
words: It was ending.

speaker: SS
words: It was ending, and then you came back and took his job?

speaker: GD
words: Well, I— he gave it to me.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. But by then most of the Greeks were gone and didn't come back?

speaker: GD
words: They didn't come back, no. There was nothing for them to come back.

speaker: SS
words: When the mill started up again, they didn't hire back too many people.

speaker: GD
words: Not very many. No.i When the mill started here, they run probably six,

speaker: GD
words: seven months a year then they shut down in the wintertime for two, three

speaker: GD
words: years up to 1940. Then they started up, and then, of course, the World

speaker: GD
words: War II come up, then they run pretty steady.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me again about your meetings with Mr. Laird?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, (Chuckles) There is nothing I can tell you. Mr. Laird was a—

speaker: SS
words: Are you thinking of an aristocrat?

speaker: GD
words: Aristocrat, that's right. Yeah, he was a aristocrat. There was one

speaker: GD
words: old fella down there and his brother-in-law was killed in the woods, in

speaker: GD
words: the fire— forest fire. And this fella had a little money tied up in

speaker: GD
words: the handkerchief, and the old man wanted to get that money to go back

speaker: GD
words: the Old Country himself, because he was too old to work here. So, he

speaker: GD
words: asked me to go down with him and talk to Mr. Laird. And his office

speaker: GD
words: then was down at the plant. They didn't have this office here, they

speaker: GD
words: didn't have this building here. SO, I says, "I go down with you", I

speaker: GD
words: says, "but I don't know whether I can make him understand." So, I went

speaker: GD
words: down there, and he took the money in the handkerchief and showed it to

speaker: GD
words: me, and he says, "That will go to his widow, because she's entitled to

speaker: GD
words: it." I don't know whether I says anything else, because, I told him

speaker: GD
words: that this man is his brother-in-law and he wants to find out what hap

speaker: GD
words: pened to the little money that he had, because he knew that he had some

speaker: GD
words: money. And that's why he took it out of his drawer there and show it.

speaker: GD
words: It belonged to the widow. And he says, things are straighten

speaker: GD
words: out here," he says, "I'll send it to the widow, and he can't get anything."

speaker: GD
words: So, I left. I didn't even know what . the widow was then, you see.

speaker: GD
words: So, I left, and, of course, the old man he sent and got a fella from Spok

speaker: GD
words: ane, he was married to his sister— this fella that died was married to

speaker: GD
words: his sister— and had. the - his- sister to give him the power of attorney,

speaker: GD
words: and that way he got the money and whatever else was awarded him for the

speaker: GD
words: death— the widow for his death.

speaker: SS
words: So it didn't go to the guy's widow?

speaker: GD
words: No, because she gave him power of attorney, this old fella— this was

speaker: GD
words: his brother, see? And, of course, he told her that was the best way and

speaker: GD
words: the easiest way to get the money is to give him power of attorney and

speaker: GD
words: to go ahead and settle the case. And he did. Another time, that I talked

speaker: GD
words: to him , I talk a little more English then, but it was up here. And I

speaker: GD
words: had to go over there and ask his secretary, secretary was downstairs,

speaker: GD
words: and she had a window, and I says, "Miss Kelly, can I see Mr. Laird?"

speaker: GD
words: You had toJask the secretary for permission to go and see, and Miss Kelly

speaker: GD
words: she call him on telephone— She says, "Greek fella," she told him, 'course

speaker: GD
words: she knew I was a Greek. "Yes," he said, "send him up." So- there was

speaker: GD
words: two of us, myself and the fella who had broke his leg. And I told him,

speaker: GD
words: "Mr. Laird,"I says, "this man had accident here in the mill and broke

speaker: GD
words: his leg. And he has been laid off now for quite a while, and he like to

speaker: GD
words: ne want to know if he can get some money to send to his family." And

speaker: GD
words: he told me then that— he says, "you tell him that we have nothing to do

speaker: GD
words: with that now. The State is taking care of that, and he'll be compen

speaker: GD
words: sated from the State when the time is ripe. He has to go through the

speaker: GD
words: State to get his money. But", he says, "if he has family and his family

speaker: GD
words: need money, why, he has some friends here, hasn't he? He can borrow

speaker: GD
words: from his friends and send to his family, and that's the only thing we

speaker: GD
words: can do for him." I think he did, I don't remember now, got some money,

speaker: GD
words: I don't know how much.

speaker: GD
words: What was Mr. Laird like to talk to?

speaker: GD
words: He was gentleman. He spoke to me pretty nice, cause I could talk a little

speaker: GD
words: English then. Some people probably had different impression. I think

speaker: GD
words: he was nice fella, to talk to.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he cared a lot about the town and the people? That he took

speaker: SS
words: a lot of care about them?

speaker: GD
words: I think he did, yes. I think he did. I think he was a humanitarian.

speaker: GD
words: He was not, you know, rough customer and didn't care for people.

speaker: SS
words: Were people afraid of him, do you think? Especially the people who were—

speaker: SS
words: didn't know the town, too good?

speaker: GD
words: They were not afraid of him, I don't think. I don't think they were

speaker: GD
words: afraid of him. There was one occasion here when the bank was owned by

speaker: GD
words: the company and the banker embezzled two hundred and fifty thousand dol

speaker: GD
words: lars from the bank. And all the Greeks, you know, they got scared and

speaker: GD
words: they rush over there to the bank to take their money, and Laird went

speaker: GD
words: over there himself, or he sent somebody else to tell the people that

speaker: GD
words: they're not going to lose their money. The company's gonna pay them every

speaker: GD
words: cents. I think he sent a fella by the name of — he himself went

speaker: GD
words: down there and told them, "Boys, you can draw your money if you want to,

speaker: GD
words: but don't be afraid, because you're not going to lose your money. The

speaker: GD
words: company's gonna pay." And it did. Paid everything. And, of course,

speaker: GD
words: they put that fella in jail. That's when the examiner came over here

speaker: GD
words: and he stay here, he stay here for quite a while.

speaker: SS
words: When you were— back in the early days when all the Greeks were liv

speaker: SS
words: ing together, pretty much. What was the social life like? What kinds

speaker: SS
words: of things did you guys do?

speaker: GD
words: Well, Our social life here was among us. We gambled sometime, but not

speaker: GD
words: very much, here in Potlatch. The social life between us and the other

speaker: GD
words: people— we live apart, you know. We didn't have too much to do— be

speaker: GD
words: cause during the War — some young fellas went down there— well, I

speaker: GD
words: was young, too, then, but I didn't go. Went down to to the gymnasium,

speaker: GD
words: they had a gymnasium here, and went down there to join, but you have to

speaker: GD
words: pay so much, you know, a small fee anyway, to be a member, and they

speaker: GD
words: wouldn't let 'em. But as far as our social life here, we had it among

speaker: GD
words: ourselves here. You know how it is. Sometimes we had somebody from

speaker: GD
words: the town come here, but not very often. And we didn't have not

speaker: GD
words: much social life— we played cards very much, pastime.

speaker: SS
words: Was that ? Oh, pinochle.

speaker: SS
words: Did guys drink very much?

speaker: GD
words: Some. Some did. Italians did, very much. They had—Over there I was

speaker: GD
words: never to them. I was living right next door and the other houses, you

speaker: GD
words: know, they were all Italians. And they had wooden balls they were playing

speaker: GD
words: with, it's something like bowling, but it was in the and they had a little ball. They

speaker: GD
words: played that in the Old Country, you

speaker: GD
words: know. In my country, too. But here they was speaking Italian, and I

speaker: GD
words: couldn t understand 'em. And pretty soon I to understand some words.

speaker: GD
words: But that's the social life everytime they— Sundays, and everytime

speaker: GD
words: they didn't — in the evenings in summertime, when the days are long,

speaker: GD
words: they were playing them balls, all the time. It was kind of funny to

speaker: GD
words: watch them. And sometimes we played, too, but not very often, because

speaker: GD
words: owned them balls, see, bought them I guess, or brought themfrom

speaker: GD
words: the Old Country, I don't where. And they drank, too.

speaker: SS
words: I heard a lot of wine came in.

speaker: GD
words: Oh, yeah. These fellas there they played for the drinks, you see.

speaker: GD
words: And there was a fella that was bootlegging, you know. And the company

speaker: GD
words: didn't allow anything like that at all. But it was private, you know,

speaker: GD
words: they didn't bother us anyway. And they drank quite a bit. He was quite

speaker: GD
words: one of 'em, he was drunk pretty near all the time.

speaker: SS
words: The Italians, did they have one man who was— who rep resented them—

speaker: SS
words: their interests to the company?

speaker: GD
words: No. No. They had one man that was foreman, the boss down to the green

speaker: GD
words: chain. And he about thirty, thirty-five men, I think, but he didn't represent

speaker: GD
words: them. He thought he did, himself, but he didn't Actually hie

speaker: GD
words: was trying to make himself known to the authorities. But I don't think

speaker: GD
words: he respresented them.

speaker: SS
words: He was just brownnosing.

speaker: GD
words: Yeah. And another thing he ordered them to strike one time. And he call,

speaker: GD
words: the strike, see. And the company give 'em— they settle up anyway. Be

speaker: GD
words: cause they on strike without notifying the company and the mill stopped.

speaker: GD
words: What happened really, the greenchain was on a strike was married man, and he had two

speaker: GD
words: daughters, one

speaker: GD
words: of 'em still living down Lewiston. But he was nice fella, he was drink

speaker: GD
words: ing too much.

speaker: SS
words: You say, he kept working when the men went on strike?

speaker: GD
words: No, no. He was the cause of the strike. He was the one that toldi, I

speaker: GD
words: think— I'm not so sure now, but I think that's what it was.

speaker: SS
words: Were many people sending part of their earnings back to the Old Country?

speaker: GD
words: Some. Some were. Some did if they had families, and of course they had

speaker: GD
words: to send 'em to support their families.

speaker: GD
words: Did I tell you about the sales they had here in the store?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard them mentioned. They were pretty big, huh?

speaker: GD
words: Well, this here was the biggest store around the Inland Empire, outside

speaker: GD
words: of Spokane. Because they were supporting all the— it was the outlet

speaker: GD
words: for the camps, you know. And there was a lot of people here, there was

speaker: GD
words: probably a thousand people working here then. And they had sales— free

speaker: GD
words: coffee and there was fellas— there was no automobiles them days, it

speaker: GD
words: was wagons. They from Palouse, oh, from all over from around the

speaker: GD
words: small towns, they come here to the sale. Oh, it was a big doings. And

speaker: GD
words: they were successful making money. But this store was the— pretty well

speaker: GD
words: supplied. You see they had every— even from from

speaker: GD
words: distance. The wagons and the buggies, whatever you call them, from all

speaker: GD
words: over. I think they were once a year, the sales. During the fall.

speaker: SS
words: The man that was the store manager, I forget his name—

speaker: GD
words: It was Mc Donald.

speaker: SS
words: He was a real wheeler-dealer, wasn't he?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. McDonald, he knew how to— he was a business man, and after he left

speaker: GD
words: why then, everything went haywire— oh, not haywire, but they didn't have

speaker: GD
words: them sales. But them sales, oh, they were— big. Big doings. Give free

speaker: GD
words: coffee. But people come from all over.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think back then that you spoke more if you were the guy that

speaker: SS
words: went with some of your friends

speaker: GD
words: That's why they asked me to represent 'em, because I could speak very

speaker: GD
words: much English. At that time, 1914, I couldn't speak very good, and that's

speaker: GD
words: why I couldn't understand Mr. Laird, what he said. Now at the office

speaker: GD
words: down there this other deal here, it was later, much later, because

speaker: GD
words: they had built a new office here. And he was up in the upstairs, you

speaker: GD
words: see, and he wasn't taking everybody— you had to ' permission from

speaker: GD
words: the secretary. Down there, was— I couldn't speak very good then, but I

speaker: GD
words: can speak a little more than the old man could, you see. And he asked

speaker: GD
words: me to, I didn't tell him that I could talk good English. But I told him

speaker: GD
words: that I'd do the best I can to help you, but if I can't— and I went down

speaker: GD
words: there, and we didn't accomplish anything because the old man wanted to

speaker: GD
words: get ahold of the money. And, of course, he got power of attorney and

speaker: GD
words: he got it anyway.

speaker: SS
words: Did you and your friends, did you take it seriously to look out for each

speaker: SS
words: other? Would you do a lot for your friends in those days? Or was it

speaker: SS
words: more every man for himself?

speaker: GD
words: Oh, well, yes, you do for friends. It was more then than it is now.

speaker: GD
words: You know, if you see a friend that you know and he's out of job, and

speaker: GD
words: wants help, why, you help him. Now, it's different, because— now, you

speaker: GD
words: see, you have all the Social Security and the Welfare and everything,

speaker: GD
words: and you tell him, "If you can't take care of yourself, go to the Welfare."

speaker: GD
words: But then you— well, you didn't have to, but it was more of the thing

speaker: GD
words: to do, than now, you see. Because you had no other way of getting money

speaker: GD
words: probably to go and look for a job— when he's out of a job or something

speaker: GD
words: like that. You do more for them then.

speaker: SS
words: Was it tough for the people to advance in the mill? To get ahead? To

speaker: SS
words: get better positions?

speaker: GD
words: It was because, I tell you, the reason was— the people that had a good

speaker: GD
words: job, they won't leave it. They stick to it. And they work them days

speaker: GD
words: as long as they could. As long as they could do the work they let 'em

speaker: GD
words: work. There was no sixty-five retirement. Which I think is alright.

speaker: GD
words: But them days they worked up to seventy, eighty 'til they died. One old

speaker: GD
words: sawyer here, he was the head sawyer, and he worked in the— sawing up

speaker: GD
words: 'til he was probably seventy years old. Then he got so old, you know,

speaker: GD
words: and he couldn't cut enough lumber and they laid him off from that job

speaker: GD
words: and they gave him cleaning up the— around the planer. And that's what

speaker: GD
words: they did them days. They got them old fellas— the only job was open

speaker: GD
words: was to clean up, and it wasn't anything that had to be done, it was just

speaker: GD
words: to give him something to do. And the wages, of course

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that there was any job discrimination against people who

speaker: SS
words: were foreigners?

speaker: GD
words: Some. Some. If they had a good job, peopledidn't like it, but then

speaker: GD
words: they no power to fire em.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: speak much English to get ahead as far as getting a higher paying posi

speaker: SS
words: tion in the mill?

speaker: GD
words: No. I don't think he can because of the language. You see, he got to

speaker: GD
words: be able to speak and understand the language. And without that, he's

speaker: GD
words: not apt to get a good job. 'Course today, it's different.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard people say, for instance, that if a foreman was a Swede you

speaker: SS
words: could expect he would favor Swedish people that came looking for a job

speaker: SS
words: over anybody else, maybe. That you could figure that would happen.

speaker: GD
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: That could make it harder for people who— let's say, if they didn't

speaker: SS
words: have a foreman of their own nationality to go to.

speaker: GD
words: That's right. That has some bearing, because he will favor his own

speaker: GD
words: til nationality. But if he don't have them, hehire someone else.

speaker: GD
words: doing the job, too. Because, he is apt to lose his own

speaker: GD
words: job if he gets people of his own nationality who don't understand or

speaker: GD
words: don't do the job. One fella told me one time that— I was grading lumber there, you

speaker: GD
words: know, for changing themsaw. And he says, "If you

speaker: GD
words: were a Swede, you could have this job. Grading." You know, grading

speaker: GD
words: lumbers, because the head grader he was a Swede, you see, and he says,

speaker: GD
words: "If you was a Swede, you can have this job." I says, "I'm not."

speaker: SS
words: I've been reading about— that there was a lot of feeling around that

speaker: SS
words: time, 1910-1920, in there, there was a lot of — they call it nativism.

speaker: SS
words: I don!t know if you ever heard them use that word. They say that there

speaker: SS
words: was a feeling a lot of people had— some people had— that was really

speaker: SS
words: anti-immigrant. They didn't want new people, foreigners coming into

words: the country at that time, some of these people. And it's funny, because

speaker: SS
words: they'd come in themselves back earlier, but now they were saying, "Well,

speaker: SS
words: if we have foreign people coming in, it's going to change the country

speaker: SS
words: in a bad way." And so, there was a lot of people that were opposed to

speaker: SS
words: immigration. And I think probably, from what I read, they say there was

speaker: SS
words: prejudice against these new people coming in, because they were changing

speaker: SS
words: their attitude about immigrants.

speaker: GD
words: There wasn't much— there wasn't very many immigrants coming in from 1920—

speaker: GD
words: 1910. 1910, yes, lot of 'em, but not in '20's. From 1910 to 1915 and

speaker: GD
words: probably— well, in 1920, some, but not too many. Not too many immigrants.

speaker: GD
words: They pttt that quota, and they can only come so many from each

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I think the quota was a part of that deal. Because they were trying

speaker: SS
words: to stop people from coming in.

speaker: GD
words: Yeah, they did. They did stop 'em. There was only a hundred people come

speaker: GD
words: from Greece. And that quota was full all the time.

speaker: SS
words: A hundred people a year?

speaker: GD
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that— do you remember there being prejudice against the

speaker: SS
words: people when they first came over here? I don't mean just Potlatch, but

speaker: SS
words: out West?

speaker: GD
words: No, because they needed them then. They needed them to build the coun

speaker: GD
words: try. And without them it wouldn't a been built. But later, there might

speaker: GD
words: have been prejudice. Well, like now, everything is built. And you get

speaker: GD
words: a bunch of foreigners— for instance like these refugees

speaker: SS
words: From Vietnam?

speaker: GD
words: Yes. I think now, I don't know. Because

speaker: GD
words: you had to give 'em something to do. You got to give 'em a job to sup

speaker: GD
words: port themselves and families, if they have them. They have families,

speaker: GD
words: too, don't they?

speaker: SS
words: But like, you say for instance here, they didn't want— they couldn't

speaker: SS
words: be in the gym at first. I heard that the Italians weren't made to feel

words: welcome at the Catholic Church, at first. When they first tried to go

words: there, so they didn't go for a while.

speaker: GD
words: Yeah. Well, I tell you, the Catholic Church— the Italians that were

speaker: GD
words: here were very few that went to church. Not because they didn't want

speaker: GD
words: , because they didn't want to go themselves. And the church, the

speaker: GD
words: Catholic Church, I don't know what they believe in, but at one time that

speaker: GD
words: fella there, that Noio , that I was telling you about being the foreman

words: on the greenchain and being the drinker, he never did go to church. And

words: when he died, they never took him to church. And these Italians that

words: were here, you know, they were mad about it, but I don't think they could

words: do anything. And I actually, I'm not Italian, but I knew this

words: fella here, and I know the Greek Orthodox Church is the same, too, but

words: they never do that, they take 'em in church anyway.

words: You mean that the town didn't do it? The church didn't do it?

speaker: GD
words: No , it was the Catholic priest. I ask him and says, "Why didn't you?"

speaker: GD
words: He says, I haty. orders from the Bishop . Boise. Whether that was

words: right or not, I don't know. The Bishop didn't know anything about this

words: man.. He was a young fella.

words: Did you—Was there a practice of the Greek Orthodox here? Did the men

speaker: SS
words: have any services here?

speaker: GD
words: No. No, it's the church in Spokane, but not here. Not here or nowhere

words: else around here.

speaker: SS
words: But you didn't get together among yourselves to have any kind of wor

speaker: SS
words: ship?

words: No. There was a priest come here one time and he stayed here for some

words: time, for two, three days, I think. And he had services here. But that's

words: a long time ago, and he never did come back.

words: Was there flny way then that the men could worship? Did they do it on

words: their own or just have to forget about it?

speaker: GD

speaker: GD
words: The church is alright, if you want to go to church it's okay, and if

speaker: GD
words: you don't— you don't have to go to church to be a good Christian. I

words: go to church here. I used to. I don't go any more because I got too

words: old, but I can't go to church in the Greek church, I go to the Presby

words: terian here.

speaker: SS
words: In the early days did the other Greeks go to the churches in town here?

speaker: SS
words: Or did they just not?

speaker: GD
words: No. Other Greeks, no. After the Greeks left, you see, it's since the

speaker: GD
words: Depression that the Greeks left here. And the last two or three, they

words: go to church if they want— once in a while. I used to go quite often.

speaker: SS
